---
title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
description: "Using ecological and network analysis to investigate the relationship between waterbird assemblages and aquatic invertebrate prey across habitat zones at a restoring coastal wetland"
author: "B. Emilio Ojemann"
date: "2026-05-13"
categories: [Research, Ecology, GIS]
image: ../../images/Devereux-Slough.jpg
---

--- 

### Overview

The North Campus Open Space (NCOS) is a large, actively restoring coastal wetland managed by the [Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration ](https://www.ccber.ucsb.edu) (CCBER), sitting just steps from the UC Santa Barbara campus. One of the main focuses of the restoration project centers on the upper arms of **Devereux Slough** and its surrounding wetland habitats, pictured below. As a Student Research Lead at CCBER, I designed and led a study investigating the spatial and trophic relationships between local waterbirds and their aquatic invertebrate prey across the distinct environments found at NCOS.

![Photograph: Bill Dewey](../../images/NCOS_SB_Audobon_Society.jpg)

--- 

### The Question

As NCOS continues to recover its historic tidal and natural connectivity, a key ecological management question emerges: how are waterbirds and their invertebrate prey distributed across the wetland, and how do habitat zones influence those patterns? Getting a good understanding of these relationships can directly inform restoration management such as deciding where to prioritize future habitat work.

---

### Methods

To investigate these patterns, I combined monthly waterbird observation data with quarterly aquatic invertebrate sampling counts across four distinct habitat zones at NCOS: the main slough channel and its mudflats; the Phelps Creek confluence; freshwater ponds; and seasonal vernal pools. Each zone is ecologically distinct (different salinities, water depths, and vegetation structures) despite being a part of the same restoration site. I then combined the specific invertebrate and waterbird communities within each zone with published aquatic diets to build **trophic networks**, or detailed diagrams of who is eating what, and where. All spatial analysis and network visualizations were carried out in ArcGIS and R.

---

### Results

The main slough channel and mudflats support the greatest waterbird diversity at NCOS, with a dense, generalist-dominated network of 25 waterbird species feeding on 9 invertebrate taxa. The Phelps Creek confluence tells a different story: Rails and Willets specialize heavily on oligochaeta worms. This saline-freshwater junction may allow for reduced competition between specialicists and generalists as it supports such a unique ecological niche. Overall, there is high overlap of waterbird species among all four zones, with the Wilson's Snipe recorded exclusively at vernal pools.

---

### Takeaways

- Food web structure, not just water presence, largely determines how waterbirds end up using restored wetland habitats.

- Fine-scale habitat monitoring captures ecologically meaningful variation that broad, site-level surveys may miss entirely. This is something restoration managers at other natural reserves can directly work towards developing.

---

### Poster
<br>
[View full poster on eScholarship](https://escholarship.org/uc/item/1bc3t7fb)
